section.title: The Paralytic and the Call of Matthew
section.sectionRationale: The miracles now provoke conflict. Jesus forgives and heals a paralytic, claiming an authority the scribes call blasphemy; He calls Matthew the tax collector and eats with sinners, scandalizing the Pharisees; and He answers a question about fasting by declaring Himself the bridegroom and His kingdom the new wine that cannot be poured into old skins. Grace and newness collide with religious resistance.
section.thesis: The Son of Man claims and proves the divine authority to forgive sins, comes as the physician of sinners who desires mercy rather than sacrifice, and reveals Himself as the bridegroom whose arrival brings a joy and newness that the old forms cannot contain.
section.frameNote: Four beats: the paralytic forgiven and healed; the call of Matthew; the meal with sinners and the word on mercy; and the question about fasting answered by the bridegroom, the patch, and the wineskins.

units.label: Your sins are forgiven
units.note: the Son of Man has authority

verses.text: Jesus got into a boat, crossed over, and came to His own town.
Just then some men brought to Him a paralytic lying on a mat. When Jesus saw their faith, He said to the paralytic, “Take courage, son; your sins are {{aphiemi|forgiven}}.”
On seeing this, some of the scribes said to themselves, “This man is blaspheming!”
But Jesus knew what they were thinking and said, “Why do you harbor evil in your hearts?
Which is easier: to say, ‘Your sins are forgiven,’ or to say, ‘Get up and walk’?
But so that you may know that the Son of Man has {{exousia|authority}} on earth to forgive sins...” Then He said to the paralytic, “Get up, pick up your mat, and go home.”
And the man got up and went home.
When the crowds saw this, they were filled with awe and glorified God, who had given such authority to men.
As Jesus went on from there, He saw a man named Matthew sitting at the tax booth. “Follow Me,” He told him, and Matthew got up and followed Him.
Later, as Jesus was dining at Matthew’s house, many tax collectors and sinners came and ate with Him and His disciples.
When the Pharisees saw this, they asked His disciples, “Why does your Teacher eat with tax collectors and sinners?”
On hearing this, Jesus said, “It is not the healthy who need a doctor, but the sick.
But go and learn what this means: ‘I desire {{eleos|mercy}}, not sacrifice.’ For I have not come to {{kaleo|call}} the righteous, but sinners.”

units.label: The bridegroom and the new wine

verses.text: Then John’s disciples came to Jesus and asked, “Why is it that we and the Pharisees fast so often, but Your disciples do not fast?”
Jesus replied, “How can the guests of the bridegroom mourn while He is with them? But the time will come when the bridegroom will be taken from them; then they will fast.
No one sews a patch of unshrunk cloth on an old garment. For the patch will pull away from the garment, and a worse tear will result.

Neither do men pour new wine into old wineskins. If they do, the skins will burst, the wine will spill, and the wineskins will be ruined. Instead, they pour new wine into new wineskins, and both are preserved.”

aphiemi.gloss: To send away, release, forgive. To forgive sins is to cancel the debt owed to God, which the scribes rightly know only God can do. Jesus' word to the paralytic is therefore a claim to divine authority, which He proves by the healing.

exousia.gloss: Authority, right, power. Jesus heals the paralytic so that all may know the Son of Man has authority on earth to forgive sins, tying the visible miracle to the invisible pardon as its proof.

eleos.gloss: Mercy, compassion, covenant kindness. Quoting Hosea 6:6, Jesus insists God desires mercy rather than mere ritual sacrifice; His table fellowship with sinners is not laxity but the very mercy God delights in.

kaleo.gloss: To call, summon, invite. Jesus came to call not the self-righteous but sinners; the word carries His gracious summons, the same call that lifted Matthew from the tax booth to discipleship.

basic.context: Back in Capernaum, friends bring a paralyzed man to Jesus, who startlingly forgives the man's sins first, then heals him to prove He has the authority to do so. He calls Matthew, a despised tax collector, and dines with tax collectors and sinners, drawing Pharisee criticism. Jesus answers that He is the doctor who came for the sick and desires mercy over sacrifice. When asked about fasting, He declares Himself the bridegroom whose presence brings joy and newness.
basic.hermeneutics: Read the paralytic scene as a deliberate claim to deity: the scribes are right that only God forgives sins, and Jesus proves His authority by the healing. Read the meal and the fasting question as revealing the character of His mission: mercy to sinners and a newness that cannot be patched onto the old. Each conflict sharpens the identity of Jesus.
basic.originalLanguages: To forgive is to cancel a debt owed to God, which is why the scribes cry blasphemy; Jesus claims that very authority and backs it with a miracle. He quotes Hosea: God desires mercy, not mere sacrifice. And He came to call sinners, the gracious summons that raised Matthew from his tax booth to follow Him.
basic.typology: Jesus as the divine forgiver fulfills the hope that God Himself would come to pardon His people. The bridegroom image draws on the Old Testament picture of God as the husband of Israel, now realized in Jesus. And the new wine signals the arrival of the new covenant that the old forms could not contain.
basic.christ: Jesus is revealed as the Son of Man who forgives sins with divine authority, the friend and physician of sinners, and the bridegroom whose coming is cause for joy. He is not adding a patch to old religion but bringing something gloriously new. And He is the one whose gracious call transforms a tax collector into a disciple and Gospel writer.
basic.god: God desires mercy, not mere ritual; He delights to forgive and to seek the sinful and the outcast. In Jesus, the God who alone can forgive draws near to pardon and heal. He is the God whose new covenant work has arrived, bringing a joy that the old wineskins of empty religion cannot hold.
basic.crossReferences: Hosea 6:6 is quoted, I desire mercy, not sacrifice. Isaiah 43 has God alone blotting out transgressions. 1 Timothy 1 says Christ came to save sinners, of whom Paul is chief. And Isaiah 62 pictures God rejoicing over His people as a bridegroom.
basic.oneStory: The King's mercy now meets open resistance, and the collision reveals who He is. He forgives sins as only God can, welcomes the very people religion despised, and announces that His coming is a wedding, not a funeral, bringing a newness the old forms cannot contain. The story moves forward as the friend of sinners gathers a people, even a tax collector, into the joy of His kingdom.

context.literary: These scenes continue the miracle section but introduce sustained conflict with the religious leaders, a rising tension that will build through the Gospel. The forgiving and healing of the paralytic, the call of Matthew, the meal, and the fasting question are linked by the theme of grace to sinners and the newness of the kingdom, and they set the stage for the mission of chapter 10.
context.historical: Tax collectors gathered tolls for Rome, were assumed to be dishonest, and were despised as collaborators and sinners; sharing a meal implied acceptance and fellowship. The Pharisees prized ritual purity and separation. Regular fasting marked serious piety. Wineskins were animal hides that stretched as wine fermented; old, brittle skins would burst under new wine.
context.canonical: Jesus' claim to forgive sins asserts a divine prerogative, Isaiah 43:25, and the crowd's response, God has given such authority to men, marvels at it. The Hosea citation, mercy over sacrifice, stands in the prophetic tradition that prizes the heart over ritual. The bridegroom and new wine imagery announce the arrival of the promised new covenant, Jeremiah 31.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show Jesus claiming and proving the divine authority to forgive sins, to reveal His mission as mercy to sinners rather than approval of the self-righteous, and to announce that His coming brings a joyful newness, the new wine of the kingdom, that the old forms cannot hold.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative describes Jesus' unique authority and mission. The call of Matthew and the word on mercy model the response of glad following and the welcome of sinners. The teaching on fasting is not a blanket rule against fasting but reveals the timing and newness of the kingdom around the bridegroom's presence.

debates.question: Does Jesus' forgiving of sins amount to a claim of deity?

views.label: Yes, He exercises a divine prerogative
views.case: The scribes rightly hold that only God can forgive sins; by pronouncing pardon and proving it with a healing, Jesus deliberately claims and demonstrates authority that belongs to God alone.

views.label: He acts as the authorized Son of Man
views.case: Jesus stresses that the Son of Man has authority on earth to forgive, exercising, as the Daniel 7 figure, an authority given Him; yet this authority is such that only God possesses it, so the claim remains one of divine identity.

debates.bearing: Both agree the episode presses Jesus' extraordinary identity: He does what only God can do, and the reader must reckon with who He is.

debates.question: What do the patch and wineskins teach about Jesus and the old order?

views.label: The kingdom brings a newness that cannot be patched onto the old
views.case: Jesus is not reforming the externals of Pharisaic religion but inaugurating the new covenant; trying to contain the new wine of His kingdom in the old skins of legalistic ritual only ruins both.

views.label: Fulfillment, not mere rejection, of the old
views.case: Jesus does not despise the Law and Prophets, which He came to fulfill; the imagery targets the brittle traditions and self-righteous forms, showing that His grace requires renewed hearts, new skins, to hold it.

debates.bearing: Both hold that Jesus brings a genuinely new work of God that demands transformed hearts, not the mere repair of old religious forms.

study.originalLanguages: When Jesus tells the paralytic his sins are aphiemi, forgiven, cancelled before God, the scribes cry blasphemy, for they know only God can do this; Jesus then proves His exousia, His authority, by the healing. Challenged for eating with sinners, He quotes Hosea, God desires eleos, mercy, not mere sacrifice, and says He came to kaleo, to call, not the righteous but sinners, the same gracious summons that raised Matthew from the tax booth. And to the question about fasting He answers as the bridegroom, whose presence turns fasting to feasting and whose new wine bursts the old skins.

typology.fulfillment: The hope that God Himself would come to blot out transgressions, Isaiah 43:25, realized in Jesus the Son of Man.
The Old Testament picture of God as Israel's husband, Isaiah 62:5, fulfilled in Jesus whose coming is a wedding celebration.

typology.type: The new wine of the kingdom
typology.fulfillment: The promised new covenant, Jeremiah 31:31-34, whose joy and grace cannot be contained by the old forms of legalistic religion.

study.christ: Jesus stands revealed as the one who does what only God can do. He forgives the paralytic's sins with a word and proves it by making him walk, so that even His enemies must weigh whether this is blasphemy or the presence of God. He is the physician who seeks the sick, the friend who eats with the despised, the Lord whose gracious call lifts a tax collector into apostleship. And He is the bridegroom, whose arrival is not an occasion for mourning but for joy, and whose kingdom is new wine, too full of life and grace to be poured into the brittle skins of self-righteous religion. In every scene He is the merciful, divine Savior of sinners.
study.god: The God of this passage delights in mercy more than in ritual and draws near in Jesus to forgive and restore. He is not the God of the scribes' suspicion or the Pharisees' separation, but the God who seeks the sick, welcomes the sinner to His table, and rejoices over His people like a bridegroom. His forgiveness is His own prerogative, now exercised by His Son, and His new-covenant work has arrived with a joy and freshness that the old wineskins of empty religion cannot hold. To know this God is to be sought, forgiven, and gladdened by His grace.

crossReferences.note: God alone blots out transgressions, the divine prerogative Jesus exercises over the paralytic.
I desire mercy, not sacrifice, quoted by Jesus to justify eating with sinners.
Christ came into the world to save sinners, the mission behind I came to call sinners.
The promised new covenant, the new wine that the old wineskins cannot contain.

study.oneStory: The King's mercy now provokes open conflict, and the clash reveals His glory. He forgives sins as only God can, calls and welcomes the outcast, and declares that His coming is a wedding rather than a fast, bringing a newness that cannot be patched onto the old. The friend of sinners is gathering a people, a tax collector among them, into the joy of the kingdom, and the story presses on toward the cross where the bridegroom will be taken away, and the empty tomb where the feast begins again.

quiz.q: Why did the scribes accuse Jesus of blasphemy when He spoke to the paralytic?

options.text: Because He claimed to forgive sins, which they knew only God can do.
options.note: Verses 2-3 show the offense: Jesus exercised a divine prerogative in pardoning sins.

options.text: Because He healed a man on the Sabbath.
options.note: That is a different conflict; here the offense was His claim to forgive sins.

options.text: Because He touched an unclean paralytic.
options.note: The charge concerned forgiving sins, not ritual uncleanness.

options.text: Because He refused to heal the man at all.
options.note: He did heal him; the offense was His prior claim to forgive sins.

quiz.q: How did Jesus prove His authority to forgive sins?

options.text: By healing the paralytic, a visible act showing His authority over the invisible one.
options.note: Verse 6 ties the healing to proving the Son of Man's authority to forgive.

options.text: By quoting Scripture to silence the scribes.
options.note: He did reason with them, but the proof He offered was the healing itself.

options.text: By calling down fire from heaven.
options.note: No such sign occurs; He healed the man as proof.

options.text: By simply insisting louder that He had the right.
options.note: He gave a real demonstration, the healing, not mere insistence.

quiz.q: Why did Jesus eat with tax collectors and sinners?

options.text: Because He came as a physician for the sick, to call sinners, desiring mercy over sacrifice.
options.note: Verses 12-13 give His reason: mercy and the call of sinners.

options.text: Because He wanted to enjoy their company and hospitality.
options.note: There was real fellowship, but His stated purpose was to seek and call sinners, not mere socializing.

options.text: Because He approved of their dishonest practices.
options.note: He called them as sinners needing mercy, not because He approved of their sin.

options.text: Because He had given up on the religious leaders entirely.
options.note: His point was the nature of His mission to sinners, not abandoning anyone out of spite.

quiz.q: How did Jesus answer the question about why His disciples did not fast?

options.text: The guests do not mourn while the bridegroom is present; His coming brings joy.
options.note: Verse 15 pictures Jesus as the bridegroom whose presence is cause for celebration.

options.text: Fasting is never appropriate for His followers.
options.note: He says they will fast when the bridegroom is taken away, so fasting has its time; this overstates.

options.text: His disciples were simply not devout enough to fast.
options.note: The reason is the bridegroom's presence, not any lack of devotion.

options.text: Fasting had been abolished by the law of Moses.
options.note: The law did not abolish fasting; Jesus points to the joy of His presence.

quiz.q: What do the patch and the wineskins illustrate?

options.text: The newness of Jesus' kingdom cannot be contained by the old, brittle forms.
options.note: Verses 16-17 show the new wine of the kingdom requires new wineskins.

options.text: That believers should discard everything from the Old Testament.
options.note: Jesus fulfills rather than discards the Law and Prophets; the images target rigid, self-righteous forms.

options.text: That old traditions are always better than new ones.
options.note: The point is the opposite; the new wine needs new skins to hold it.

options.text: That Jesus came to make no changes at all.
options.note: The imagery announces a genuinely new work that the old forms cannot hold.

questions: Jesus addressed the paralytic's deepest need, forgiveness, before his obvious need of healing. What surface need are you focused on, and what deeper need might Jesus want to meet first?
Matthew got up from his tax booth and followed at a word. Is there something Jesus is calling you to leave behind in order to follow Him more fully?
Jesus said He desires mercy, not sacrifice, and welcomed sinners to His table. Who are the people your circles tend to exclude, and how might you show them the mercy Jesus shows?
The Pharisees were scandalized that Jesus ate with sinners. Do you ever resent grace being shown to people you think do not deserve it, and how does your own need of mercy reframe that?
Jesus is the bridegroom whose presence turns fasting into feasting. How does knowing Him as a source of deep joy, not just duty, change the way you follow Him?

translationNotes.note: The sentence breaks off mid-thought, so that you may know the Son of Man has authority to forgive sins, then turns to command the healing; the interrupted grammar dramatizes the proof about to be given.
I desire mercy, not sacrifice quotes Hosea 6:6; some later manuscripts add to repentance after but sinners, as the BSB footnote notes.
Wineskins were animal hides; new wine still fermenting would stretch and burst old, brittle skins, illustrating the incompatibility of the kingdom's newness with rigid old forms.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Matthew 9:1-17. Verse text assembled by concatenating segment values in order; line-break and note markers were skipped. The ellipsis in verse 6 stands in the source text. No em or en dashes appear. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
